subject: pure logic tests
claim: Vitest's default Node environment is the correct fast boundary for timeline math, reducers, serialization, and command construction that do not require browser behavior.
evidence_quote: node is default environment
impact: Pure logic can be exhaustive and fast without carrying DOM or browser flake.
implication: Create a Node-only project for deterministic model logic; negative control: mutate a frame/time calculation or serialized command and require this tier to fail.
notes: Do not add jsdom merely because production code is imported by React; extract browser-free logic instead.

subject: jsdom boundary
claim: jsdom cannot prove layout, rendering, navigation, canvas pixels, media playback, WebGL, WebCodecs, or worker scheduling because it does not implement visual layout/rendering and uses dummy behavior for major browser features.
evidence_quote: jsdom still does not do any layout or rendering
impact: A passing jsdom test can conceal exactly the media and visual defects Vean is most exposed to.
implication: Limit jsdom to DOM/state contracts; negative control: break an element's measured geometry or canvas draw while keeping DOM structure and confirm jsdom still passes but a browser test fails.
notes: Mocks of getBoundingClientRect, HTMLMediaElement, canvas, or WebCodecs assert the mock contract, not WKWebView behavior.

subject: React component behavior
claim: React component tests should use role, accessible name, and label queries before test IDs so their interaction contract tracks user-visible semantics.
evidence_quote: getByRole ... should be your top preference
impact: These tests simultaneously defend interaction wiring and basic accessible naming without binding to component internals.
implication: Adopt role/name queries for controls and timeline actions; negative control: remove an accessible label while leaving the click handler intact and require the test to fail.
notes: This is necessary but not a substitute for a real accessibility-tree or assistive-technology pass.

subject: React async updates
claim: React assertions must occur after updates are flushed through act-compatible helpers; Testing Library already wraps its helpers with act.
evidence_quote: apply pending React updates before making assertions
impact: Immediate assertions or arbitrary sleeps can pass against stale UI or create timing-dependent failures.
implication: Use awaited user interactions and find/wait queries; negative control: defer the state update to a microtask and confirm an un-awaited test fails while the correct awaited test passes.
notes: Prefer framework helpers over direct act boilerplate.

subject: effect lifecycle
claim: React Strict Mode deliberately re-renders and re-runs effects and ref callbacks in development to expose impurity and missing cleanup.
evidence_quote: re-run Effects an extra time
impact: Vean media listeners, timers, workers, and Player buffer handles are especially vulnerable to duplicated subscriptions and leaked resources.
implication: Mount lifecycle-sensitive components under StrictMode in tests; negative control: remove an event-listener or worker cleanup and require duplicate delivery or leak detection to fail.
notes: StrictMode behavior is development-only, but it detects real lifecycle bugs.

subject: browser component tests
claim: Vitest Browser Mode runs tests in an actual browser and supports separate Node and browser projects, making it the appropriate tier for CSS layout, canvas, media APIs, WebGL, WebAudio, WebCodecs, and workers.
evidence_quote: run your tests in the browser natively
impact: Vean can retain Vitest ergonomics while moving browser-dependent proof out of jsdom.
implication: Create explicit browser test globs/projects; negative control: call a genuine browser API or assert nonzero layout and require the browser project to fail when the implementation is removed.
notes: A browser-mode test is still not a packaged Tauri/WKWebView test.

subject: browser interaction fidelity
claim: Vitest warns that its preview provider and direct simulated user-event path are not suitable CI proof; Playwright or WebdriverIO providers drive interactions through the browser driver.
evidence_quote: to run tests in CI ... playwright or webdriverio
impact: Synthetic dispatch can bypass actionability, focus, pointer, keyboard, and browser policy behavior.
implication: Use a driver-backed provider and vitest/browser userEvent; negative control: cover a disabled, obscured, or unfocusable control that synthetic dispatch would activate but the driver must reject.
notes: This is a direct warning against treating event mocks as real input.

subject: WebCodecs capability
claim: WebCodecs support is runtime-, codec-, configuration-, and device-dependent, so availability stubs cannot establish that Vean's actual codec path works.
evidence_quote: isConfigSupported ... supported by the User Agent
impact: Codec success in Chromium CI may differ from macOS WKWebView, hardware, OS version, and source media.
implication: Probe isConfigSupported and decode representative fixtures in real browsers plus WKWebView; negative control: request an unsupported codec/profile or corrupt chunk and require explicit fallback/error behavior.
notes: Feature presence alone is weaker than configuration and fixture proof.

subject: WebCodecs lifecycle
claim: VideoFrame and AudioData hold scarce CPU/GPU resources and should be closed promptly; codecs may also be reclaimed under resource pressure.
evidence_quote: SHOULD be released immediately when no longer in use
impact: Short happy-path tests can pass while sustained scrubbing or preview eventually exhausts memory or hardware decoder capacity.
implication: Add long-loop decode/scrub tests with frame and decoder cleanup assertions plus memory/queue ceilings; negative control: omit frame.close() and require the stress budget to fail.
notes: A mocked VideoFrame.close cannot reveal real GPU pressure.

subject: worker message boundaries
claim: Worker messages use structured cloning and transfer semantics; transferred objects become unusable in the sender and unserializable values throw DataCloneError.
evidence_quote: transferred, not just cloned
impact: Directly calling worker functions or mocking postMessage misses ownership, serialization, race, and termination failures.
implication: Exercise real Worker instances and transferable buffers/frames in browser tests; negative control: reuse a transferred buffer or send a non-cloneable value and require the real boundary to fail predictably.
notes: Keep pure worker algorithms unit-tested separately, then test the actual protocol in browser.

subject: WebGL lifecycle
claim: After WebGL context restoration, prior textures and buffers are invalid and the application must recreate its rendering state.
evidence_quote: resources ... created before ... are no longer valid
impact: A visual editor can appear correct initially but go blank after GPU reset, sleep/wake, pressure, or renderer recovery.
implication: Use WEBGL_lose_context in a browser test and assert redraw after restoration; negative control: skip resource recreation and require the restored-frame pixel assertion to fail.
notes: jsdom and mocked WebGL cannot prove this lifecycle.

subject: audio correctness
claim: OfflineAudioContext is the deterministic boundary for validating audio graph output, while AudioContext is real-time and separately subject to device and autoplay behavior.
evidence_quote: AudioContext (for real-time rendering) and OfflineAudioContext (for offline rendering)
impact: Sample-level audio math can be stable in CI without pretending it proves speakers, timing, resume policy, or WKWebView playback.
implication: Golden selected offline sample windows and separately test live AudioContext state in browser/WKWebView; negative control: alter gain/routing and require the offline buffer diff to fail.
notes: Do not replace live audio proof with only OfflineAudioContext.

subject: media autoplay
claim: Audible HTML media and Web Audio commonly require a real user gesture, and autoplay policy differs by browser and state.
evidence_quote: playback will likely be blocked
impact: Calling play() on a mocked media element produces false confidence about Vean preview audio.
implication: Driver-click the actual play control and assert play promise/state/audio context; negative control: remove gesture propagation or call play on load and require the policy test to expose blocked playback.
notes: Actual WKWebView remains the final authority for the shipped macOS app.

subject: Remotion component frames
claim: Remotion components are regular React components with extra contexts, and Thumbnail can render them at an exact frame for fast structural assertions.
evidence_quote: show the component at a specific frame
impact: Frame-dependent branches and prop mapping can be tested cheaply without mounting a playing Player.
implication: Use Thumbnail for representative boundary frames and semantic assertions; negative control: introduce an off-by-one frame condition and require the boundary-frame test to fail.
notes: Happy DOM or SSR output does not prove pixels, media decode, animation pacing, or Player controls.

subject: Remotion Player behavior
claim: Player correctness includes buffering transitions, waiting/resume events, seek cleanup, and four distinct playing/buffering states that static component rendering cannot exercise.
evidence_quote: it emits the waiting event ... emits the resume event
impact: A frame looks correct while playback can stall, advance during buffering, leak handles, or show the wrong control state.
implication: Drive a real Player with delayed media, seek during buffering, and assert events/time/control UI; negative control: omit unblock cleanup or advance time while buffering and require failure.
notes: Use deterministic local media fixtures and controlled delayed responses rather than public network media.

subject: Remotion media policy
claim: Remotion discourages Player autoPlay for compositions with audio and documents Safari-specific gesture propagation and nondeterministic autoplay restrictions.
evidence_quote: autoPlay ... discouraged if your video contains any audio
impact: A Chromium-only or mocked Player test can miss shipped WKWebView audio failures.
implication: Test explicit user-start and autoplay failure fallback in actual WKWebView; negative control: move play outside the captured user event and require audio-state/fallback evidence to fail.
notes: Remotion notes policy can depend on battery saver or thermal pressure, so graceful failure is part of correctness.

subject: render readiness
claim: delayRender controls server-side frame capture readiness but is a no-op in Player/Studio preview; preview buffering requires useBufferState separately.
evidence_quote: It has no effect ... in ... Studio or Player
impact: A render test can pass while interactive preview flickers or runs ahead of unloaded content, and vice versa.
implication: Maintain separate render-readiness and Player-buffering tests; negative control: remove delayRender and require renderStill to capture incomplete content, then remove delayPlayback and require Player timing to fail.
notes: One API cannot cover both boundaries.

subject: deterministic frame output
claim: renderStill renders a specified zero-indexed composition frame to an image and is the strongest production-render-path primitive for frame goldens.
evidence_quote: Renders a single frame to an image
impact: DOM snapshots cannot detect typography, transforms, masks, compositing, canvas, and raster regressions in exported frames.
implication: Render curated first/middle/last and transition-boundary frames with fixed props/assets; negative control: change a visible transform/color or frame boundary and require the image comparison to fail.
notes: Keep this distinct from screenshots of the interactive Player chrome.

subject: visual regression stability
claim: Browser screenshots vary with OS, browser version, fonts, hardware, power, and headless mode, so visual baselines must be generated and compared in a pinned identical environment.
evidence_quote: run tests in the same environment
impact: Unpinned goldens either flap or acquire thresholds so loose that real visual regressions pass.
implication: Pin runner image, browser, fonts, viewport, scale, color mode, reduced motion, and assets; negative control: shift a stable element by a few pixels and ensure the configured threshold fails.
notes: Use separate baselines for materially different browser/platform projects.

subject: accessibility assurance
claim: Axe in browser catches common machine-detectable violations but official guidance says many accessibility failures require manual assessment and inclusive testing.
evidence_quote: many accessibility problems ... only ... manual testing
impact: Zero axe violations cannot prove timeline keyboard operation, focus order, announcements, macOS VoiceOver behavior, or media control usability.
implication: Run axe on important states plus keyboard/focus assertions and periodic VoiceOver checks in packaged Tauri; negative control: remove a label for axe failure and separately break keyboard-only reachability for interaction failure.
notes: Avoid broad axe exclusions; they suppress every rule for descendants.

subject: HMR reliability
claim: React Fast Refresh is a development-only module-boundary contract and can invalidate or propagate when files mix incompatible exports.
evidence_quote: file should only export React components
impact: Vean's edit loop can silently degrade to full reload, losing player/timeline state even though production tests pass.
implication: Add a dev-server HMR smoke that edits a component and proves state preservation plus no full reload; negative control: introduce an incompatible mixed export and require the HMR-state test to fail.
notes: A production build or unit test does not exercise Vite's HMR graph.

subject: production bundle
claim: Vite production builds have a distinct bundle, target, asset rewriting, mode, and lack of automatic polyfills, so passing dev-server tests does not prove the artifact embedded by Tauri.
evidence_quote: Vite only handles syntax transforms and does not cover polyfills
impact: Asset URLs, worker chunks, dynamic imports, environment values, and WKWebView syntax/API support can differ only after build.
implication: Run browser smoke against fresh dist and then packaged Tauri; negative control: break base-relative asset or worker URL resolution and require the production-artifact test to fail while dev remains green.
notes: vite preview is only a local static preview, not the final Tauri protocol/host.

subject: WebKit coverage
claim: Playwright's WebKit is a patched build derived from WebKit main and is not branded Safari or the exact OS WKWebView embedded by Tauri.
evidence_quote: Playwright doesn't work with the branded version of Safari
impact: Playwright WebKit is valuable early compatibility coverage but cannot close Vean's shipped-renderer, codec, native protocol, autoplay, or process-lifecycle risk.
implication: Use Chromium plus macOS Playwright WebKit for fast browser coverage, then require a smaller actual Tauri/WKWebView suite; negative control: exercise a Tauri protocol/native bridge or WKWebView-only media case that standalone WebKit cannot satisfy.
notes: Run Playwright WebKit on macOS for closer video behavior, but do not label it WKWebView proof.

subject: media codec matrix
claim: Playwright documents that codec availability varies by browser build and operating system, and its bundled Chromium omits some codecs present in branded Chrome/Edge.
evidence_quote: available media codecs vary substantially
impact: A single Linux Chromium lane can reject media Vean supports on macOS or accept only a nonrepresentative subset.
implication: Define a small representative media corpus and execute capability/decode checks per supported engine/OS, with WKWebView as macOS release authority; negative control: include one deliberately unsupported profile and verify graceful classification rather than blanket success.
notes: Matrix assertions should record supported/unsupported outcomes, not assume universal parity.

subject: performance budgets
claim: CI can fail against predefined performance criteria, but Vean's useful budgets must measure editor-specific interactions and media paths rather than relying only on a generic Lighthouse score.
evidence_quote: fail builds if they don't meet pre-defined criteria
impact: Regressions in scrub-to-paint latency, dropped frames, long tasks, memory, decode queues, and startup may not change functional assertions.
implication: Budget production-build startup, representative scrub input-to-frame, dropped/late frames, long tasks, memory, and worker/decoder queues on pinned hardware; negative control: inject a blocking loop or leak and require a named budget to fail.
notes: Lighthouse is useful for bundle/resource regressions but is not designed around a desktop NLE interaction model.

source_title: Synthesized boundary from official Vitest, Remotion, Playwright, and Web standards
subject: Vean assurance model
claim: No single React/browser tool can prove Vean: assurance must layer Node logic, semantic React components, real browser APIs, deterministic Remotion frames, cross-browser compatibility, and actual packaged WKWebView/native-shell checks.
evidence_quote: separate configurations for different testing strategies
impact: Collapsing layers into jsdom mocks or one E2E suite creates blind spots or slow, flaky feedback.
implication: Make every roadmap lane name its boundary and negative control; final completion must run post-integration proof at all layers, with actual WKWebView reserved for risks that browser runners cannot prove.
notes: Recommended split: Node pure logic; jsdom only simple DOM; Vitest Browser/Playwright for Web APIs; renderStill for frame goldens; axe plus manual/VoiceOver; Tauri WebdriverIO and packaged smoke for release truth.
